texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Introducing new technologies into the market is always a challenging process requiring various reference test trials with long-term operation under real conditions. In this particular case the industrial feasibility of membrane distillation is evaluated choosing an application of industrial relevance for the development of a pre-industrial unit. In a structured investigation the gap between laboratory investigations and material contemplations to full-scale module and plant design is carried out.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In general, membrane distillation is a thermal separation technology which is capable of serving various separation needs. First described in the 1960s by Bodell (1968) and Weyl (1967), since then there have been plenty of applications described for MD in literature. The most promising amongst them are the desalination of seawater, process water or the further concentration of brine from reverse osmosis units and the recovery of highly pure valuable compounds from liquid mixtures (Wang and Chung, 2015).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The operation of pre-industrial membrane distillation (MD) units is of great importance in order to implement this new technology. The systematic development from module to plant design for MD is conducted in an industrial research-cooperation. The consequent up-scaling from a lab plant at Hamburg University of Technology (TUHH) for MD module evaluation to a pre-industrial plant is presented. Permeate gap MD (PGMD) and Direct Contact MD (DCMD) spiral wound modules with different channel lengths have been used in this study to evaluate the most suitable module configuration and design. It has been decided to go along with DCMD modules with a relatively short channel length. A pilot plant for on land testing has been built and operated for 9 months before constructing the pre-industrial unit. In the pre-industrial plant waste heat was used and a heat recovery and recycle concept was implemented and tested successfully. It has been proven that long-term operation with real seawater is possible without significant performance decline. Besides operational data also MD plant design specifics will be discussed. The importance of ambient pressure conditions for the MD modules was determined. In addition, the possibility of piping system deaeration is discussed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work the focus is on the application of MD for the desalination of seawater in order to demonstrate its industrial feasibility. Seawater is available on earth in large quantities. 96.5% of total water is contained in World's Oceans (Gleick, 1993). Only few water resources can be called freshwater. But freshwater is necessary and extensively used for industrial processes and agriculture. Furthermore, freshwater is unexceptional to satisfy drinking water needs of world's population. Traditional freshwater resources such as lakes, rivers and groundwater are not capable of satisfying the existing and steadily growing demand. New areas of freshwater generation such as water reuse and desalination had to be developed. Today, desalination has already become an important part of freshwater provision.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.1. State of the art
texts.text: 1.1. State of the art

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Desalination technologies can be divided into thermal desalination and membrane separation processes. Thermal desalination processes are already known for more than 150 years (Al-Shammiri and Safar, 1999) and have been developed to large-scale multi-stage flash evaporation plants (MSF) over the past 60 years (Greenlee et al., 2009). In contrast, membrane processes with the most commonly used reverse osmosis process (RO) developed over the past 40 years and yet have overtaken thermal desalination in terms of installed capacity. In 2015 a total worldwide installed capacity of desalination plants of 86.5 million m 3 /d was reported from which 65% are based on the RO principle and 21% on MSF processes. From all feed water types used in these desalination plants 59% of those are using seawater and 22% are using brackish water (Virgili et al., 2016).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Both, membrane processes and thermal processes have certain disadvantages which shall be overcome by using a new technology either instead or as a supplement. RO membrane processes need to overcome the osmotic pressure which is increasing with the salinity of the feed medium. Therefore, a relatively high electrical energy demand is necessary to operate the reverse osmosis unit with seawater or brine. Known evaporators need to have a large number of stages and therefore a high space demand in order to run at a low thermal energy demand. Furthermore, in regards of the waste heat temperature needed, vacuum is used to lower the boiling point.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A large variety of MD experiments in small-scale laboratory applications have been reported in literature showing its significance in science. Furthermore, it has already been shown that MD is feasible for decentralised small-scale applications with available solar energy (Manna et al., 2010; Fath et al., 2008; Summers and Lienhard, 2013; Ong et al., 2012; Banat et al., 2007a,b, 2002; Qtaishat and Banat, 2013; Koschikowski et al., 2009) as well as in industrial plants for further brine concentration (Mariah et al., 2006; Kayvani Fard et al., 2015; Nguyen and Lee, 2015; Minier-Matar et al., 2014; Geng et al., 2015; Duong et al., 2015a; Ji et al., 2010; Guan et al., 2015). An overview on solar powered standalone MD systems is given in Khayet (2013), Saffarini et al. (2012). Some hints towards practical implementation of MD are given in Susanto (2011). Further pilot tests have been carried out with marine salt solutions or seawater (Jansen et al., 2013; Minier-Matar et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2013; Zaragoza et al., 2014; Raluy et al., 2012; Schwantes et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2006; Guillén-Burrieza et al., 2012, 2011). However, there is a lack of systematic investigations from module to plant design followed by long-term operation tests. This work will cover the MD process from a small scale laboratory test unit to larger plant behaviour.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Small scale behaviour such as stream and heat transfer both play an important role in the MD process. They need to be known to understand the characteristics of the different MD configurations and to be able to decide for the most suitable configuration for a given separation task. These different MD configurations and different MD module construction opportunities will be explained briefly. Finally, characteristic parameters for the evaluation of modules and plant operation will be presented.

texts.text: 2.1. Mass & heat transfer

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In membrane distillation a membrane separates two fluids from each other (with the exception for the vacuum configuration, where vacuum is applied at the distillate side). One of these has to be a liquid, the other one can either be a liquid or a gas. In the simplest configuration a liquid is present on both sides of the membrane. Here, due to the temperature difference of the two liquids a temperature gradient over the membrane is established. Water vapour permeates through the membrane due to the temperature dependent partial water vapour pressure difference (see Fig. 1). Furthermore, besides the diffusive mass transfer j M,d a convective mass transfer j M,conv occurs if an absolute pressure difference across the membrane is applied (Souhaimi and Matsuura, 2011).
texts.text: In membrane distillation a membrane separates two fluids from each other (with the exception for the vacuum configuration, where vacuum is applied at the distillate side). One of these has to be a liquid, the other one can either be a liquid or a gas. In the simplest configuration a liquid is present on both sides of the membrane. Here, due to the temperature difference of the two liquids a temperature gradient over the membrane is established. Water vapour permeates through the membrane due to the temperature dependent partial water vapour pressure difference (see Fig. 1). Furthermore, besides the diffusive mass transfer j M,d a convective mass transfer j M,conv occurs if an absolute pressure difference across the membrane is applied (Souhaimi and Matsuura, 2011).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. MD configurations
texts.text: 2.2. MD configurations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Different configurations for MD exist introducing a third channel in the system or having a gaseous flow on one side of the membrane. Commonly used configurations are named direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD), permeate gap membrane distillation (PGMD), air gap membrane distillation (AGMD), vacuum membrane distillation (VMD) and sweeping gas membrane distillation (SGMD). Detailed descriptions are given by Lawson and Lloyd (1997, 1996a,b) and Souhaimi and Matsuura (2011). Each MD configuration has its own advantages and disadvantages compared to the other ones. So the most suitable configuration depending on the separation task should be chosen.

texts.orig: 2.3. Module design
texts.text: 2.3. Module design

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two different kinds of membranes can be used in MD, either flat sheet or hollow fibre membranes. Modules consisting of flat sheet membranes can be in the form of plate-and-frame or spiral wound modules. Reviews (Drioli et al., 2015; El-Bourawi et al., 2006) give an overview on MD technology and module configuration. In this study the focus is on spiral wound MD modules because they show a high packing density and the channel length can be easily used to influence the performance characteristics.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to do the module design, the process itself has to be understood fully and the most suitable MD configuration has to be chosen beforehand. Materials for MD modules play an important role in minimising heat losses, pressure drops and module leakages and can increase long-term stability. For further module developments a membrane and spacer screening was conducted in order to find a suitable material and determine the influences of the characteristic specifications. A publication that covers this topic has already been submitted (Hagedorn et al., 2017). Another publication on module design specifics has been submitted as well (Winter et al., 2017).

texts.orig: 3.1. Spiral wound membrane distillation modules
texts.text: 3.1. Spiral wound membrane distillation modules

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Modules used in the described pilot plants are produced by the Fraunhofer Institute for Solar Energy Systems (ISE). The modules contain a hydrophobic microporous ePTFE membrane. In order to establish a separate permeate channel in PGMD modules, a thinner PP spacer with a thickness of 0.25 mm and an impermeable ethylene tetrafluoroethylene (ETFE) film of 127 𝛍 m thickness are used. The hot water channel is referred to as feed or evaporator channel whereas the cold water channel is referred to as condenser channel. The fluids on both sides of the membrane are in counter current flow. A spacer in the channels establishes the flow channel. Further detailed information on the fabrication of PGMD modules can be found in Winter et al. (2011).

The hot water channel is referred to as feed or evaporator channel whereas the cold water channel is referred to as condenser channel. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 3 illustrates the compact spiral package of the module which similarly applies for PGMD and DCMD modules, independently from flow channel length. Salty feed water is passing the module from (3) to (4), while water vapor and heat is extracted from the feed, transferred through the membrane and released on the permeate side. In a DCMD module, the condenser channel from (2) to (6) is operated with recirculating pure product water. In a PGMD module, permeate is separated from the condenser channel by an impermeable polymer film (8) so that salty feed water may be directly preheated within the module. Modules with a channel length of several meters require relatively thick spacers in order to avoid pressure drops that increase pumping costs significantly or that are higher than the liquid entry pressure (LEP) of the membrane leading to wetting. According to the established nomenclature of the 'Round Table' at the 'Workshop on Membrane Distillation' in 1986: 'The liquid entry pressure of water is the pressure that must be applied onto water before it penetrates into a non-wetted (dry) membrane' (Smolders and Franken, 1989).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the systematic evaluation of MD plants the MD module itself was studied first. A lab plant at TUHH was used for these pre-investigations. Systematic experiments enabled the understanding of the fundamental MD process behaviour. MD modules with DCMD and PGMD configuration and different geometries (varying channel width and channel length) as well as different operating points (e.g. feed salinity, temperature and volume flow) were investigated in detail.

texts.orig: The lab plant at TUHH was built in 2012 by the Fraunhofer ISE for the evaluation of PGMD spiral wound modules with a production capacity up to 1 m 3 freshwater per day. It was extended in 2013 for the evaluation of DCMD modules. A schematic diagram of the lab plant is shown in Fig. 4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The lab plant is equipped with a heating circuit driven by electricity (9). Furthermore, the plant is connected to the cooling system of the University (5). This allows the inlet temperatures to be ensured in the module on the hot and cold side. All pipes are made of plastic (either PVC-U or PP depending on the expected temperatures of the fluid). The feed is introduced into a storage vessel (180 l) (2) and then pumped through the system. The retentate is re-entering the feed vessel, whereas the permeate is collected separately (7). After a certain time interval the permeate is pumped back into the feed vessel in order to avoid concentration of salt.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Iwaki Direct Drive Pumps RD-40 are used to circulate up to two fluid flows (3). In order to protect the spiral wound membrane distillation module from any particles, two filters (4) are integrated in the feed circuit (sieve filter, grade 50 𝛍 m and cartridge filter, grade 10 𝛍 m). The volume flow of the two fluid circuits is measured using a magnetic flow meter (KROHNE, Optiflux 5100) (6). For temperature measuring at the channel inlets and outlets Pt100 sensors are used. The temperature sensors are directly located in front/after the module inlet/outlet. For safety reasons the pressure drop over the module channel is measured. The pressure drop should not exceed 1 bar. The produced permeate is weighted using a Soehnle Professional 2790 with an accuracy of 1 g. For PGMD modules the permeate can be collected separately and weighted directly. In case of DCMD operation the permeate is directly mixing with the recirculating freshwater. Therefore, the weight of the overflow water in the condensate storage vessel is determined. In order to depict seawater, artificial seawater is prepared according to Kester et al. (1967).

texts.orig: To evaluate the characteristic performance, different steady state operating conditions are measured, analysed and evaluated. Each operating point is driven for at least 90 min. From this time period the arithmetic average values of the last 30 min are used for the evaluation. To compare the performance of different operating points, characteristic values with practical relevance are compared. These process parameters of interest are the flux J and the specific thermal energy demand q th . Furthermore, the electrical conductivity of feed and permeate are recorded to ensure no wetting has occurred and the feed specifications are constant.

texts.orig: 3.3. Pilot plant Helgoland
texts.text: 3.3. Pilot plant Helgoland

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After pre-investigations on the MD process and the module design have been completed, in the next step a pilot plant was designed, constructed and operated under real conditions. The focus of this pilot plant was to operate continuously over a long period of time under real conditions (e.g. seawater as feed medium). For this reason new modules were not yet tested in this pilot plant because downtime due to module failure should be avoided. Instead the well-known PGMD modules were used here.
texts.text: After pre-investigations on the MD process and the module design have been completed, in the next step a pilot plant was designed, constructed and operated under real conditions. The focus of this pilot plant was to operate continuously over a long period of time under real conditions (e.g. seawater as feed medium). For this reason new modules were not yet tested in this pilot plant because downtime due to module failure should be avoided. Instead the well-known PGMD modules were used here.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A pilot plant with a capacity of 2 m 3 freshwater per day at its design point has been installed on the island Helgoland in the North Sea and operated for 9 months. It was continuously operated with seawater. Two times two modules with PGMD configuration have been installed (module #05-#08, Table 1), each line with a different kind of pre-filtration. Specifications of the modules can be found in Table 1. Said pilot plant has been operated with an electrical heating circuit due to lack of available waste heat. Owing to a requirement for simplicity in construction, heat exchangers (11) in line A and B have been using the same heating circuit. A parallel flow through was realised with a 3-way valve which can be adjusted so hot water is distributed equally to the two lines. The output is set to a fixed value during commissioning and can be adjusted manually if heat demand in the two lines is unequal. The produced permeate was not considered for further use and was disposed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A picture of the pilot plant Helgoland can be seen in Fig. 6, a schematic diagram in Fig. 5. Seawater is sucked in close to the coast line through the pumping system of the existing waterworks on Helgoland. Part of this seawater is stored into an intermediate bulk container (IBC) from where the feed water pumps (3) of the pilot plant suck the water into the system. Pre-filtration of line A is equipped with a gravel filter (5) and two cartridge filters (6) having a filtration grade of 1-2 mm, 50 𝛍 m and 10 𝛍 m, respectively. The other line B is equipped with an automatic backflush filter (7) and also two cartridge filters (6) having a filtration grade of 50 𝛍 m, 50 𝛍 m and 10 𝛍 m, respectively. In front of the pre-filtration system there is a sulfuric acid dosing unit (2) as pre-treatment measure which guarantees a pH of 6.3 in order to keep scaling effects in the modules low. The original pH of seawater was between 7.6 and 8 depending on the season. In Warsinger et al. (2015) potential reasons for scaling and its mitigation in MD are intensively discussed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A production capacity of 5 m 3 /d can be achieved with this pilot plant. Two DCMD desalination modules, namely module #11 and #12 (Table 1), are installed. Heat source for the MD desalination modules is the waste heat of the diesel generators. No additional thermal energy input is needed. The pre-industrial unit is operated with the seawater from a feed pump which is also supplying the existing freshwater generator on board. No additional feed pump is installed with the pre-industrial unit since the supply pressure is sufficiently high for operation. The plant is equipped with a circulation pump in the condenser circuit. A pump for 'seawater out' and 'product out' is needed to overcome backpressure of the existing system. The produced permeate together with the product water of the freshwater generator was used on board for cleaning, washing, showering and replacement water in the cooling circuit. A schematic diagram of the pre-industrial plant concept can be seen in Fig. 12.

texts.orig: 4.1.1. Comparison of module configurations
texts.text: 4.1.1. Comparison of module configurations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: DCMD and PGMD configuration were compared in order to find the most suitable module design for the pre-industrial plant. For comparison of DCMD and PGMD configuration various operating points of the modules #01 and #02 were evaluated using the lab plant TUHH described in Section 3.2 and expanded by simulation results. In order to evaluate the characteristic performance of a module, in general, the flux and the specific thermal energy demand play an important role. Modules can be designed in a way that they have either a high flux or a low specific energy demand. For the specific application a high flux module is advantageous over a low energy demand module because the available heat amount on board needs to be cooled down anyway.
texts.text: DCMD and PGMD configuration were compared in order to find the most suitable module design for the pre-industrial plant. For comparison of DCMD and PGMD configuration various operating points of the modules #01 and #02 were evaluated using the lab plant TUHH described in Section 3.2 and expanded by simulation results. In order to evaluate the characteristic performance of a module, in general, the flux and the specific thermal energy demand play an important role. Modules can be designed in a way that they have either a high flux or a low specific energy demand. For the specific application a high flux module is advantageous over a low energy demand module because the available heat amount on board needs to be cooled down anyway.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A simulation study has been performed with a simulation model developed and described by Winter (2015). The flux in dependence of the evaporator channel inlet temperature is depicted in Fig. 8 for a DCMD and a PGMD module with tap water and artificial seawater as feed medium. Modules with a channel length of 1 and 7 m, respectively, have been compared. In order to make these modules comparable the same turbulence must be generated in the flow channel. This is achieved through identical flow velocities which means the volume flows are different since the channel width is different. Backing orientation of membrane is towards condenser channel for DCMD and towards evaporator channel for PGMD. Influence of backing orientation was extensively studied by Winter et al. (2013). According to the data presented in the reference the orientation of the membrane backing towards the cold side (condenser channel) leads to a higher flux. For the given conditions a flux increase of about 10% can be expected for backing facing the condenser channel in PGMD modules compared to facing the evaporator channel.

For the given conditions a flux increase of about 10% can be expected for backing facing the condenser channel in PGMD modules compared to facing the evaporator channel.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the simulation study the highest flux of 13.7 kg/(m 2 h) (12.8 kg/(m 2 h) in the experiment) was achieved for a DCMD module with tap water and a channel length of 1 m. The possible flux to be achieved is higher for modules with a shorter channel. In general, the simulation study leads to higher fluxes than measured in the experiments. The deviation from the simulation is less than 5.6% for PGMD and lower than 16% for DCMD.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As can be seen in Fig. 8 the flux increases in the DCMD and the PGMD module for higher evaporator channel inlet temperatures T ei . This is expected because of the higher temperature gradient across the membrane introduced by the higher temperature difference between the two inlet channel temperatures. A flux increase for the DCMD configuration compared to the PGMD configuration is more significant for modules with a channel length of 1 m than for modules with a channel length of 7 m. For the given module configurations a flux increase between 46% with T ei = 80 ◦ C and 61% with T ei = 60 ◦ C can be expected for DCMD modules with a channel length of 1 m compared to PGMD modules with the same channel length. Here, the increase has to be lowered by the backing orientation effect. So with the same backing orientation a flux increase of roughly 32-45 % can be expected.

texts.orig: It can be concluded that the DCMD configuration gives a higher flux compared to PGMD. As a third channel and an impermeable film in PGMD decreases the relevant temperature gradient influencing the driving force. Furthermore, the quality of the permeate does not differ from PGMD to DCMD configuration and is in general below 10 𝛍 S/cm for tap water and below 20 𝛍 S/cm for artificial seawater.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1.2. Upscaling of DCMD modules
texts.text: 4.1.2. Upscaling of DCMD modules

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In general, there are three possible ways to increase the capacity of a plant, either multiply the process steps and parallelise them or upscale the process steps. The upscaling of MD modules is prioritised over increasing the amount of modules in one plant. Modules can be up scaled by increasing the channel width (which is directly influencing the total module height) or increasing the number of parallel feed flow channels. The module capacity shall be increased up to a size one module can still be handled of two people in case of maintenance. Furthermore, it has to be considered that membrane and spacer materials need to be available in the required dimensions otherwise module height can be limited due to material specifications. In addition, the hydrostatic pressure of the water column has to be considered and can be a limiting factor for upscaling the module height as well. Finally, it has to be secured that the distribution over the channel width is uniform.
texts.text: In general, there are three possible ways to increase the capacity of a plant, either multiply the process steps and parallelise them or upscale the process steps. The upscaling of MD modules is prioritised over increasing the amount of modules in one plant. Modules can be up scaled by increasing the channel width (which is directly influencing the total module height) or increasing the number of parallel feed flow channels. The module capacity shall be increased up to a size one module can still be handled of two people in case of maintenance. Furthermore, it has to be considered that membrane and spacer materials need to be available in the required dimensions otherwise module height can be limited due to material specifications. In addition, the hydrostatic pressure of the water column has to be considered and can be a limiting factor for upscaling the module height as well. Finally, it has to be secured that the distribution over the channel width is uniform.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the first step the channel width has been increased. Then the modules consisting of only one feed channel have been expanded to six parallel feed channels in one module. Here, a suitable distribution structure needs to be designed. In order to evaluate the possible module height, modules with different channel width have been compared. The aim was to find out about a uniform or non-uniform flow distribution over the channel width which would result in an unequal flux of the modules. As the membrane and spacer used in the module were available with a dimension of 1 m this was the maximum channel width. In Fig. 9 module #02-#04 with a channel width of b , 2 b and 3 b , respectively, are compared. It could have been expected that there are more distinct dead-zones in the larger module due to worse mixing behaviour and therefore the flux would have been lower than for a smaller module. Apparently this is not the case here. The larger module #04 is showing a higher flux at the same spacer-filled channel flow velocity for low velocities. The difference between the flux of the smaller and the larger module under the same conditions becomes less for higher flow velocities. No difference can be seen comparing module #02 and #03 at an evaporator channel inlet temperature of 80 ◦ C. It is concluded that no flow limitations are given for the module channel width (below 1 m) since the flux is behaving identical which indicates a good mixing in the flow channel.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1.3. Variation of flow velocity and volume flow ratio
texts.text: 4.1.3. Variation of flow velocity and volume flow ratio

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In DCMD two fluid circuits are operated. Usually the volume flows in both channels are equal corresponding to a volume flow ratio of /CAN = 1 resulting in a nearly symmetric temperature profile of both channels. Different volume flows lead to different flow velocities which have an influence on the mixing behaviour. Furthermore, that leads to an increased or decreased ability of the flow to emit or incorporate heat based on their individual thermal capacities described by the product of ˙ m · c P for each channel. This means that also the /Delta1 T between inlet and outlet of each channel has to be different. Overall this leads to an asymmetric behaviour of the temperature profiles. A detailed study describing such behaviour was recently published by the authors (Winter et al., 2017).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In general, an increase in permeate mass flow with increasing flow velocity is attributed to a higher thermal capacity ˙ m · c P as well as a better mixing behaviour in the bulk fluid flow meaning the boundary layer between fluid and membrane surface becomes smaller. In addition, the temperature gradients T ei -T co and T eo -T ci across the membrane differ due to a change in outlet temperatures at constant inlet temperatures. E.g. for /CAN > 1 (volume flow in condenser channel higher than in evaporator channel) the outlet temperature of the condenser channel and the evaporator channel is lower compared to the case /CAN = 1. For gaining a higher permeate flow than for /CAN = 1 a volume flow ratio of /CAN > 1 is advantageous over /CAN < 1 meaning a high temperature gradient for T ei -T co is more advantageous than a high temperature gradient for T eo -T ci .

texts.orig: In the lab plant only a cooling device CX was used to ensure the temperature T ci instead of an additional heat recovery exchanger RX. Nevertheless, the theoretical /Delta1 T HX with a heat recovery concept can be derived from these results. Taking into account the specific thermal energy demand, the effect of /CAN / = 1 changes. In Fig. 11 the temperature difference /Delta1 T HX is shown for different volume flow ratios /CAN . /Delta1 T HX was calculated according to Eqs. (7)-(10) and is an indication for the energy demand in the external heat exchanger. It can be seen, that the temperature difference is increasing with increasing feed flow velocity thus leading to a higher external heat demand ˙ QHX . Furthermore, the lowest temperature difference is achieved with /CAN = 1. As described earlier /CAN / = 1 always leads to an asymmetric temperature profile which adversely affects the external heat demand. These results correspond to the findings in Winter (2015).

texts.orig: 4.2.1. MD plant design
texts.text: 4.2.1. MD plant design

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After the pre-investigations at TUHH, the pilot plant design was thought through. In the following, MD plant design will be focussing mainly on plants comprising DCMD and PGMD spiral wound modules although some aspects might be applied for other configurations as well.
texts.text: After the pre-investigations at TUHH, the pilot plant design was thought through. In the following, MD plant design will be focussing mainly on plants comprising DCMD and PGMD spiral wound modules although some aspects might be applied for other configurations as well.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The core component of a MD plant is of course the MD module itself. Different to other membrane separation processes pressure in the modules has to be kept at a moderate level to prevent membrane mechanical stress and potential wetting. The operation at ambient pressure makes it possible to run the process with a small feed pump or even without any pump if the provision of feed water is at moderately elevated pressure starting from pressure levels of about 0.5 bar overpressure. Due to the pressure sensitivity of the MD modules it is strongly recommended to undock pre-treatment and MD module part via a pressure reducing valve and thus guarantee a constant pressure at the module inlet. In case of DCMD operation (with two fluid streams) also the pressure difference between both channels should be kept low to reduce mechanical stress and wetting potential of the membrane. As pressure in the modules is kept at slightly elevated ambient pressure it should be noticed that following process steps must only have a small pressure drop or the outlet stream is directly discharged via open-end or temporarily stored in a storage tank before further processing.

texts.orig: In order to minimise the risk of a two-phase-flow in the modules due to degassing of soluble gases with increasing temperatures, a deaeration by an air bleeding valve is useful. Even a total degassing of the feed stream can further increase the flux but as a vacuum pump is needed it would increase the investment costs and plant complexity as well as the energy demand of the plant. It has been shown that production rate can be maintained at a higher level when partially or fully degassing the feed stream (Winter et al., 2012).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In case of spiral wound modules the length of the channel influences the amount of internal heat recovery in the module itself. Figuratively, the internal heat recovery can be compared to that of a MSF process which is commonly used for seawater desalination. Modules built by Fraunhofer ISE are especially designed for low energy demand operation of MD. With the PGMD configuration the feed water is preheated while passing the long module channel of 7 m thus giving the hot feed the necessary temperature gradient for evaporating water through the membrane and collecting it in the third separate permeate channel. Low specific thermal energy demands below 170 kWh/m 3 and 190 kWh/m 3 have been experimentally measured within this study using module #01 for tap water and artifical seawater, respectively. As the temperature difference between feed water inlet and feed water outlet for those modules is insignificant, an additional external heat recovery is not useful. As intended in this module configuration, heat is already recovered inside the module.

texts.orig: With a DCMD configuration a separate third channel is not available as explained in Section 2.2. Therefore, the feed water cannot be used for internal heat recovery in the module. Here, an external heat recovery makes sense as it was already introduced in Fig. 2. Excess heat ˙ QRX can be recovered from the condenser channel outlet. Furthermore, the freshwater in the condenser circuit needs to be cooled down by means of ˙ QCX after passing the module in order to be able to run it in cycle operation. Hence, a cooler is needed.

texts.orig: hot feed water inlet are required. Here, the feed flow is controlling the feed pump speed or the feed water supply valve, the heat supply valve is controlled via the 'feed hot' temperature. Quality of the product water can be measured online via monitoring the electrical conductivity. An increase in electrical conductivity during stationary operation is an indication for a leakage and thus a failure in module integrity. A sign of significant fouling in the MD modules is an increasing pressure drop over the module channel during stationary operation. Besides the increase in pressure drop a decrease in production rate might also be observed. In conclusion, as monitoring device an electrical conductivity sensor in the product stream as well as a pressure sensor in front of the feed water inlet of the module is recommended. It is proposed to choose a maximum T ei of 90 ◦ C and an inlet pressure of maximum 1 bar.

texts.orig: Another part of practical relevance is concerning the pretreatment needs in MD plants. The pre-treatment concept for the pilot plant Helgoland was already described in Hagedorn et al. (2014a,b). Large pre-treatment would lead to an increased overall plant complexity and investment costs. Both prefiltration methods show good results in terms of module performance. None of the module channels became blocked during overall operation time which would have been indicated by an increasing pressure drop. Backflush filter as well as gravel filter is automatically rinsed. Here, backflush filter is rinsing when pressure difference over the filter reaches a certain level whereas gravel filter is rinsing after a certain amount of water has passed through the filter. Rinsing or backflushing always causes the feed water supply to the MD modules to fluctuate during cleaning. This is not desirable but occurring for both lines. The use of only cartridge filters would be advantageous but could lead to an increased maintenance. Cartridge filter elements in both lines do not show a significant difference compared to each other in visual inspection, so first step of pre-filtration does not have a very different influence.

texts.orig: Based on the experiences in operating the pilot plant Helgoland and an approach proposed by Fane et al. (1987) a similar concept for DCMD modules was developed and tested. This concept is depicted in Fig. 12 and is combining internal heat recovery and stream recycle.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In standard DCMD operation the product water on the cold side of the membrane is recirculated through the module and the permeate produced is taken up by the circulating water. Usually a buffer tank is included in this circuit which is emptied from time to time when it filled up completely because of the additional water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Due to the fact (see Section 4.2) that the water on the cold side of the membrane is heating up while passing the module a cooling device is needed in order to be able to recirculate the water. It is proposed to operate the plant with a heat exchanger RX. With this approach a cooling device is not needed for this application and component complexity is reduced. RX transfers excess heat from the condenser circuit to the feed water. One advantage is the saving of a cooling device and a second one the preheating of the feed water. By this arrangement an internal heat recovery in the plant is realised.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As typical a second heat exchanger HX is needed to heat the feed water to the desired 'feed hot' temperature. Because the seawater temperature normally is not constant when the ship is on its way due to the heat recovery via RX a varying seawater temperature would cause a differing 'feed warm' temperature as well as a differing module condenser channel inlet temperature. A stationary operation of the DCMD module would not be possible and the production rate of the unit would be dependent on the seawater temperature. Therefore, a stream recycling via recycling part of the 'seawater out' was implemented. The aim of the stream recovery was on the one hand to increase the recovery ratio of the system and on the other hand to guarantee a constant 'feed mix' temperature. When a recycle stream is present and a constant volume flow to the module is desired, the 'seawater in' volume flow has to be reduced by the amount of the recycle stream in order to keep the 'feed mix' volume flow constant.

The seawater supply volume flow is controlled via the total feed volume flow and is reduced according to the recycle stream.

texts.orig: A further improvement is the usage of state of the art PLC hardware. A PLC programme has been developed which is operating the plant at its set point and shutting down if a problem occurs. A start up and shut down procedure is implemented to ensure proper operation. In general during start up procedure of the plant firstly the volume flow of feed and condensate circuit is set to a specified value. Afterwards 'feed hot' temperature is set and antiscalant dosing unit is turned on. When volume flows and temperature of 'feed hot' is steady a value for the 'feed mix' temperature is set and recycling is started.

texts.orig: The modules used in the pre-industrial plant are DCMD modules with six parallel flow channels with a channel length of 3.1 m and a channel width of 0.74 m. The DCMD configuration was chosen as it was concluded from pre-investigations that DCMD gives a higher flux compared to PGMD. For the planned application on board a high flux module is advantageous over a low energy demand module. Furthermore, because the modules should achieve high throughput the channel width should be as high as possible. It was shown in Section 4.1.2 that upscaling of DCMD modules is possible without flow limitations. For proper manufacturing of the new modules, a maximum channel width of 0.74 m could be achieved from the available 1 m wide membrane and spacer material. In Section 4.1.1 a comparison between modules with a channel length of 1 and 7 m was carried out. Further simulation studies for a channel length between 1 and 10 m showed that an acceptable flux and a moderate specific thermal energy

For proper manufacturing of the new modules, a maximum channel width of 0.74 m could be achieved from the available 1 m wide membrane and spacer material. In Section 4.1.1 a comparison between modules with a channel length of 1 and 7 m was carried out. texts.label: text
texts.orig: At a given seawater temperature of 25 ◦ C a variation of recycle ratio R has been performed which leads to a variation of 'feed mix' temperature. As can be seen in Fig. 17 the influence on the recovery ratio is by far the greatest. The specific thermal energy demand is only changing about 5% at the highest simulated recycle ratio of 0.6 whereas the production rate drops by 23% because of the increasing condenser channel inlet temperature T ci and thus the decreasing temperature gradient across the membrane. The specific thermal energy demand stays relatively constant because the heat demand in HX is decreasing as well because the 'feed mix' temperature is increasing because of the recycle stream and thus the 'feed warm' temperature is increased. The influence of the recycle stream on the feed salinity is not very high in the considered region (increase from 35 g/kg to maximum 38 g/kg). In conclusion, a recycle ratio of 0.4 for this given operating point can be recommended as the production rate only drops by 10% whereas the recovery ratio increases by 50%. The effect of recovery ratio increase with recycle ratio is more significant at low seawater temperatures and furthermore the production rate does not decrease as much then.

texts.orig: Compared to the results obtained using the pilot plant Helgoland it can be mentioned that the flux achieved here for a comparable operating point (feed volume flow 3.5 m 3 /h, T ci = 23.2 ◦ C, T ei = 70 ◦ C) is 54% higher (1.7 l/(m 2 h)). At the design point of the pre-industrial unit a flux of 3.7 l/(m 2 h) has been measured. Results for the optimised specific thermal energy demand are limited due to the little results with feed recycle but are surely higher than the 207 kWh/m 3 from the pilot plant Helgoland because the channel is shorter. Nevertheless, with feed pre-heating the additional waste heat amount needed can be significantly reduced and thus the specific thermal energy demand.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study a seawater desalination plant using MD was developed and operated under real conditions. In the first step an evaluation concerning the most suitable module configuration has been taken place. MD modules with DCMD and PGMD configuration and different channel length and channel width have been investigated, analysed and compared in the lab plant TUHH. DCMD modules were showing a higher flux compared to PGMD modules, therefore, this configuration has been chosen for further investigations. The influence of the volume flow ratio on flux and specific thermal energy demand in DCMD operation was studied. It was proven that a volume flow ratio /CAN / = 1 is influencing both flux and specific energy demand. In order to develop large modules an upscaling study was performed to reveal possible flow limitations. No limitations could be determined under sufficiently high flow velocities. Based on the gained experiences, the module design has been re-worked and more parallel flow channels have been introduced in one module.
texts.text: In this study a seawater desalination plant using MD was developed and operated under real conditions. In the first step an evaluation concerning the most suitable module configuration has been taken place. MD modules with DCMD and PGMD configuration and different channel length and channel width have been investigated, analysed and compared in the lab plant TUHH. DCMD modules were showing a higher flux compared to PGMD modules, therefore, this configuration has been chosen for further investigations. The influence of the volume flow ratio on flux and specific thermal energy demand in DCMD operation was studied. It was proven that a volume flow ratio /CAN / = 1 is influencing both flux and specific energy demand. In order to develop large modules an upscaling study was performed to reveal possible flow limitations. No limitations could be determined under sufficiently high flow velocities. Based on the gained experiences, the module design has been re-worked and more parallel flow channels have been introduced in one module.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A first pilot plant desalinating seawater was operated for nine months to gain experiences under real conditions. The most important design aspects in MD plant design have been discussed. An operation of the MD modules at ambient pressures is desired and pressure peaks should be avoided. Air bubbles due to degassing at elevated temperatures are influencing the performance. During the operation no significant performance decline could be observed in the pilot plant ashore.
texts.text: A first pilot plant desalinating seawater was operated for nine months to gain experiences under real conditions. The most important design aspects in MD plant design have been discussed. An operation of the MD modules at ambient pressures is desired and pressure peaks should be avoided. Air bubbles due to degassing at elevated temperatures are influencing the performance. During the operation no significant performance decline could be observed in the pilot plant ashore.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A pre-industrial unit for on board a ship operation has been designed. A heat recovery and recycle concept was implemented in order to lower the specific energy demand and to increase the recovery ratio. Furthermore, an attempt was made to reach an independent operation from seawater temperature. Commissioning of the pre-industrial unit was completed successfully. Various data for different operating conditions have been recorded. The feasibility of heat recovery and feed recycle has been proven.
texts.text: A pre-industrial unit for on board a ship operation has been designed. A heat recovery and recycle concept was implemented in order to lower the specific energy demand and to increase the recovery ratio. Furthermore, an attempt was made to reach an independent operation from seawater temperature. Commissioning of the pre-industrial unit was completed successfully. Various data for different operating conditions have been recorded. The feasibility of heat recovery and feed recycle has been proven.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study an important step towards industrial application of MD plants has been made. It was proven that long-term operation is possible without experiencing significant performance decline.
